claim: For all integers n, gcd(n^2+1, n+1) divides 2.
gcd(n^2+1, n+1) equals 2 iff n is odd, and 1 iff n is even.
n^2+1 is never divisible by 4.
n+1 and n^2+1 are both even exactly when n is odd.
If n is odd, gcd(n^2+1,n+1)=2 and (n^2+1)/2 and (n+1)/2 are not both even.
gcd(n^2+1, n-1) divides 2.
gcd(n^2+1,n-1) equals 2 iff n is odd, and 1 iff n is even.
gcd(n^2+1, 2n+1) divides 5.
gcd(n^2+1, n^2-1) divides 2.
gcd(n^2+1,n^2-1) equals 2 iff n is odd.
For n odd, (n^2+1)/2 is never divisible by 4.
n^2+1 is divisible by 5 iff n is congruent to 2 or 3 mod 5.
n^2+1 divisible by 5 implies n^2+1 not divisible by 25 when n mod 25 lies in the set giving exactly one factor of 5 (i.e. vp(n^2+1,5) equals 1 for those residues).
gcd(n^2+1,4) never equals 4.
gcd(n^2+1, 2*(n+1)) divides 4.
n+1 divides n^3+1 for all integers n.
gcd(n^3+1, n^2+1) divides 2.
gcd(n^2+n+1, n+1) equals 1 for all integers n.
gcd(n^2+n+1, n^2+1) equals 1 for all integers n.
gcd(n^2+1, n^2-n+1) divides 2.
For n even, both gcd(n^2+1,n+1) and gcd(n^2+1,n-1) equal 1 simultaneously.
For n odd, the triple gcd(n+1, n-1, n^2+1) equals 2.
n^2+1 is never divisible by 20.
n^2+1 divisible by 10 iff n congruent to 3 or 7 mod 10.
phi(n^2+1) is even for all n>2.
n^2+1 is never a perfect square for n>0.
tau(n^2+1) is even for all n>0.
gcd(n^3+1,n^2+1) equals 2 iff n is odd, and 1 iff n is even.
The triple gcd(n^2+1, n^2+n+1, n+1) equals 1 for all n.
For n odd, gcd(n^2+1, n^2-1) equals the triple gcd(n+1,n-1,n^2+1), both equal to 2.
vp(n^2+1,2) is at most 1 for all n.
n^2+1 divisible by 5 and n odd implies (n^2+1)/2 divisible by 5.
The sequence gcd(n^2+1,n+1) has period 2 in n: gcd(n^2+1,n+1) equals gcd((n+2)^2+1,(n+2)+1).
The sequence gcd(n^2+1,n-1) also has period 2 in n.
n^4+1 is never a perfect square for n != 0.
tau(n^4+1) is even for all n>0.
gcd(n^2+1, n^2+2) equals 1 for all n.
gcd(n^2+2, n^2-1) divides 3 for all n.
For n odd, (n^2+1)/2 is odd iff n congruent to 1 mod 4.
For n congruent to 3 mod 4, vp((n^2+1)/2, 2) equals 1.
gcd(n^2+1, n+1) is never divisible by any odd prime, for all n.
gcd(n^2+1, n+1) is a power of 2 for all n (equivalently 1 or 2).
For n congruent to 1 mod 4, n^2+1 is congruent to 2 mod 4.
For n congruent to 3 mod 4, n^2+1 is congruent to 2 mod 4.
For all odd n, n^2+1 is congruent to 2 mod 4, unifying both residue classes mod 4.
For all odd n>1, n^2+1 has at least two distinct prime factors.
predicate: (n%2==0 or n<=1) or (len(set([p for p in range(2,n**2+2) if (n**2+1)%p==0 and is_prime(p)]))>=2 or (n**2+1)==2)

claim: gcd(n^2+1, 2n+1) equals 5 iff n is congruent to 2 mod 5.
gcd(n^2+1, n^2-n+1) equals 2 iff n is odd.
gcd(n^3+1, n^2+1) always divides gcd(n^2+1, n+1), so it too divides 2 with the same parity characterization confirmed independently via n^3+1 factorization.
For all odd n, n^2+1 equals 2 times an odd number whose tau (divisor count) is even, hence tau(n^2+1) equals 2*tau((n^2+1)/2).
For n congruent to 3 or 7 mod 10 (so n odd and n^2+1 divisible by 10), (n^2+1)/2 is divisible by 5 and n^2+1 is congruent to 2 mod 4 simultaneously, giving vp(n^2+1,2)=1 and vp(n^2+1,5)>=1 together.
